persona.name: Julian Vane
persona.bio: Master tailor specializing in period construction and bespoke theatrical costume fabrication. Known for impeccable hand-stitching, traditional Savile Row techniques, and structural engineering for performance durability. 12 years building costumes for West End theater and major film productions.

images.title: Professional Headshot
images.description: Studio portrait - character reference for all identity-based images
images.prompt: Professional studio portrait of a 35-year-old British-Nigerian man, professional first hand tailor, short buzz cut, clean-shaven, medium-dark brown skin tone, focused confident gaze, wearing crisp white collared shirt, soft studio lighting against neutral gray background, professional headshot photography, 85mm lens, sharp detail

images.title: Workshop Selfie
images.description: Casual moment in the tailoring workshop during a build period
images.prompt: Generate a photo in the style of an iPhone front camera snapshot. British-Nigerian man with medium-dark brown skin and short buzz cut centered in frame looking directly at the lens with casual friendly expression, wearing work apron over t-shirt, measuring tape around neck, positioned close to camera as if holding it at arm's length. Tailoring workshop visible behind him with cutting table and fabric bolts. Natural workshop lighting from windows. Camera positioned at chest level pointing slightly upward at his face. The composition and lighting match what an iPhone front-facing camera captures in a casual indoor selfie, but the image shows ONLY what the camera lens sees - the person and background. The phone taking the photo is not visible in the resulting image. No hands holding phone visible. No camera interface, buttons, or UI elements. No timestamp or date overlays. Clean photograph from the camera's perspective.

images.title: Candid Workshop Shot
images.description: Behind-the-scenes candid of Julian working at his cutting table
images.prompt: Candid photo of Julian Vane working at large cutting table, shot by workshop colleague, British-Nigerian man with medium-dark brown skin and short buzz cut focused on precision cutting fabric with professional shears, wearing work apron, pattern pieces and rulers visible on table, measuring tools, professional tailoring workshop environment, natural daylight from windows, authentic work documentation

images.title: Fitting Session
images.description: Julian conducting a fitting, demonstrating his construction expertise
images.prompt: Julian Vane during live fitting session, shot by colleague, British-Nigerian man with medium-dark brown skin and short buzz cut actively pinning fabric adjustments on actor wearing half-finished garment, measuring tape draped over shoulder, pin cushion on wrist, professional workshop setting, captured mid-action showing expertise and precision

categories.name: Menswear Tailoring
categories.description: Traditional Savile Row construction techniques applied to period and contemporary menswear. Hand pad-stitching, canvas work, and precision fitting for theatrical and bespoke projects.

categoryContent.headline: Traditional Tailoring for Stage and Screen
categoryContent.introduction: Menswear tailoring demands precision, patience, and deep knowledge of structural construction. Every jacket I build receives hours of hand pad-stitching, carefully engineered canvas layers, and multiple fittings to achieve both period-accurate silhouettes and modern performance durability.
categoryContent.approach: I follow traditional Savile Row methods: hand-drafted patterns, horsehair canvas interfacing, pad-stitched lapels, and hand-sewn buttonholes. Each garment is built to last 100+ performances while maintaining crisp lines and perfect drape.

projects.title: Bespoke Morning Coat
projects.description: Traditional formal morning coat requiring exacting Savile Row construction standards. Built for a period drama production demanding authentic Victorian tailoring techniques. The coat featured hand-stitched peak lapels with perfect roll, internal canvas architecture for shoulder structure, and hand-sewn buttonholes. Construction time: 80 hours over 4 weeks.

projectDetails.timeline: 4 weeks from first pattern to final press

projectContent.challenge: Achieving authentic Victorian silhouette while ensuring durability for 40+ performances. The morning coat's distinctive peak lapels required extensive hand pad-stitching to achieve proper roll without collapse during wear.
projectContent.approach: Hand-drafted pattern from historical references, traditional canvas construction with horsehair interlining, 12 hours of pad-stitching on lapels and fronts, hand-sewn buttonholes, and steam-pressed shaping.
projectContent.outcome: The coat maintained its crisp silhouette through the entire run, with the director specifically noting the authenticity of movement and drape during performance.

challenges: Balancing historical accuracy with performance durability
Fitting across multiple body positions (sitting, standing, movement)

projects.title: The Diplomat's Suit
projects.description: Three-piece charcoal suit built for lead character in contemporary political drama. Required modern fit with traditional construction methods for camera-ready appearance and durability across 60+ filming days. Full canvas construction with hand-finished details throughout. This project led to an unexpected commission - a real diplomat who visited the production was so impressed with the construction quality that he commissioned a personal suit using the same techniques.

projectContent.challenge: Building a modern suit that would read perfectly on camera in close-up while maintaining traditional construction integrity. The production required the suit to look sharp across 60+ filming days with no visible wear.
projectContent.outcome: The suit's construction quality caught the attention of a visiting diplomat who later commissioned a personal three-piece suit. The film's costume designer credited the tailoring as 'invisible perfection' - exactly what camera-ready work demands.

categories.name: Theater Production
categories.description: Construction work for West End and regional theater productions. From pattern drafting through tech rehearsals, documenting the complete build process for principal and ensemble costumes.

categoryContent.headline: Building Costumes for the Stage
categoryContent.introduction: Theater construction demands both historical accuracy and extreme durability. Each costume must withstand 40+ performances while maintaining its silhouette and details under harsh stage lighting and intense physical movement.
categoryContent.approach: I document every build from first pattern draft through opening night. My phone captures the construction details - the patterns laid out, the fabric cut and ready, the garment taking shape on the form. Production photographers capture the finished work under lights.

projects.title: The Obsidian Crown
projects.description: Dark fantasy tragedy requiring costume deterioration effects across the run. Built 28 principal costumes and 50+ ensemble pieces. Lead construction on Queen's gown with progressive darkening effects, Officer's coat with hidden armor structure, and multiple courtier ensembles with distressed embroidery.

projectDetails.timeline: 12 weeks from first pattern to opening night

techniques: Structured canvas work for period silhouettes
Hidden LED integration for curse effects

projects.title: Much Ado About Nothing: 1960s Miami
projects.description: Contemporary reimagining of Shakespeare's comedy set in 1960s Miami. Built 18 principal costumes requiring period-accurate 1960s silhouettes with tropical color palette and lightweight fabrics for outdoor summer performances. Lead construction on Beatrice's mod shift dress with geometric trim and Benedick's linen suit.

projects.title: The Negative Space
projects.description: Contemporary experimental black box production exploring grief and memory through minimalist costume design. Built 6 principal costumes with transformation elements allowing actors to shift between past and present. Lead construction on reversible coat revealing inner emotional state, deconstructed shirt with exposed seam work as design element, and modular ensemble pieces that reconfigured throughout the 90-minute one-act.

projectDetails.venue: The Pit - Black Box Theater, London
projectDetails.timeline: 6 weeks build with ongoing rehearsal collaboration

projectContent.challenge: Building costumes that could physically transform during performance without quick-change booths. Each garment needed to function in multiple configurations while maintaining the minimalist aesthetic. Exposed construction elements became design features rather than hidden work.
projectContent.approach: Deconstructed tailoring where seams, darts, and internal structure became visible design elements. Reversible construction techniques. Modular pieces that could be worn multiple ways. Everything needed to transform silently on stage.
projectContent.outcome: The costumes became active participants in the storytelling. Critics noted the 'haunting minimalism' of the costume work. The reversible coat technique has since been adopted by other designers for similar experimental work.

techniques: Exposed seam work as design element

categories.name: Film Production
categories.description: Costume construction for film and television. Building for camera requires different finishing techniques than theater - everything must be perfect in close-up, continuity across multiple shooting days is critical, and stunts require duplicate builds.

categoryContent.headline: Building for the Camera
categoryContent.introduction: Film construction demands invisible perfection. Every seam, every button, every stitch must be flawless because the camera sees everything. Continuity across weeks of shooting means meticulous documentation and sometimes building multiple identical copies of the same garment.
categoryContent.approach: I photograph every stage of construction - not just for my records, but because the continuity department needs documentation. My phone documents the build stages; the unit photographer captures the work on set under lights.

projects.title: The Crimson Inheritance
projects.description: Period mystery drama set in 1880s England exploring family secrets and inheritance disputes. Six-month shoot requiring historically accurate Victorian construction. Built 12 principal character costumes with multiple copies for stunts and continuity. Lead construction on female lead's burgundy silk day dress with 30+ buttonholes and male lead's formal three-piece suit.

projectDetails.location: UK Location Shoot - Yorkshire and London
projectDetails.timeline: 16 weeks pre-production build, 6 months on-set support
projectDetails.scale: 12 principal costumes, 40+ duplicates and specialty builds

techniques: Spiral steel boning in corsets and bodices
Stunt double construction and continuity matching

projects.title: Shadows and Smoke
projects.description: Neo-noir crime thriller set in 1940s Los Angeles with contemporary edge. Eight-month shoot requiring period film noir construction with modern cinematic finishing. Built 15 principal costumes emphasizing sharp tailoring, dramatic silhouettes, and high-contrast fabric choices for black-and-white aesthetic despite color filming. Lead construction on detective's signature double-breasted overcoat, femme fatale's bias-cut evening gowns, and gangster three-piece suits.

projectDetails.timeline: 14 weeks pre-production build, 8 months production support
projectDetails.scale: 15 principal costumes, 30+ duplicates for stunts and continuity

projectContent.challenge: Creating costumes that would read dramatically in the director's high-contrast lighting style. Every fabric choice had to consider how it would appear under harsh key lighting and deep shadows. The detective's overcoat needed to move beautifully for the noir aesthetic while surviving stunt work.
projectContent.approach: Heavy emphasis on silhouette and drape over surface decoration. Structured shoulders, crisp lapels, perfect pressing. Fabrics chosen for how they absorbed or reflected light. Built three identical copies of the detective's hero coat for stunts, continuity, and backup.
projectContent.outcome: The costume work became integral to the film's visual style. The director specifically lit scenes to showcase the coat's movement. Film festival reviews noted the 'impeccable period tailoring' as enhancing the noir atmosphere.

techniques: Structured shoulder pads for noir silhouettes
High-contrast fabric selection for dramatic lighting

categories.name: Specialty Techniques
categories.description: Advanced construction skills beyond standard tailoring: leatherwork, corsetry, and armor fabrication. These techniques showcase the breadth of skills required in theatrical costume construction, often combining multiple crafts in a single project.

categoryContent.headline: Mastering Specialized Construction
categoryContent.introduction: Theatrical costume construction demands expertise beyond traditional tailoring. Leatherwork requires different tools, different stitching techniques, different finishing. Corsetry demands precision engineering for both historical accuracy and performer comfort. Each specialty technique is a craft unto itself.
categoryContent.approach: I document these techniques extensively - the tools, the materials, the finished elements. These builds often span multiple projects, showing technique development across different applications.

projects.title: Leatherwork
projects.description: Leather construction techniques across multiple projects: armor pieces, period accessories, and structural costume elements. Working with vegetable-tanned leather, brass hardware, hand-tooling, and weathering effects. This showcase includes work from post-apocalyptic armor to historical leather doublets.

projects.title: Corsetry
projects.description: Period corsetry construction for theater and film. Building historically accurate foundation garments with modern comfort considerations for 8+ hour performance wear. Techniques include spiral steel boning, hand-set grommets, and traditional coutil construction. Work shown spans Victorian, Edwardian, and Regency periods.
